source_metadata.title_raw: Christopher J. Diaz -I-19-210 - January 13, 2020 - Walnut Creek

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

January 13, 2020

Christopher J. Diaz
City Attorney
Town of Hillsborough
for BEST BEST & KRIEGER LLP
2001 N. Main Street, Suite 390
Walnut Creek, CA 94596

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. 1-19-210

Dear Mr. Diaz:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of
the Political Reform Act (the "Act") on behalf of the Town of Hillsborough ("Town").¹ Please note
that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest code and Statement of Economic
Interest reporting provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions
such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090.

Please note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for
additional advice. Because your inquiry is general in nature, we are treating your request as one for
informal assistance.²

QUESTIONS

1. Has the Town properly designated the Architecture & Design Review Board (ADRB) and
the Administrative Hearing Panel (AHP) in the Town's conflict of interest code?

2. Has the Town properly classified volunteer members of its Financial Advisory Committee
(FAC) as Section 87200 filers?

3. For public officials designated in the Town's conflict of interest code, what constitutes

___________________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

File No. I-19-210
Page No. 2

a "business contact" for purposes of determining an official's reportable investment
interests?

CONCLUSIONS

1. Yes. Because the ADRB and the AHP possess decision-making authority, their members
must be designated in their agency conflict of interest codes.

2. Yes. Because the FAC is comprised of officials who manage public investments, FAC
members are properly classified as Section 87200 filers.

3. While "business contacts" do not include merely marketing via the Internet, telephone,
television, radio, or printed media, if a company does business on a regular and substantial
basis with a resident or other persons with a physical presence in the Town, this could
constitute "doing business in the jurisdiction."

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

Your firm serves as City Attorney to the Town of Hillsborough ("the Town"). As previously
noted in the Kasten Advice Letter (I-05-037), the Town does not have commercial zoning or places
of business; it is strictly a residential community consisting of single-family homes, four public
schools, two private schools, and two private clubs. The Town is governed by a City Council,
which is assisted in carrying out its governance functions by about twenty resident volunteers, who
sit on various boards and committees, including the Financial Advisory Committee ("FAC"), the
Architecture & Design Review Board ("ADRB"), and the Code Enforcement Hearing Panel or
Administrative Hearing Panel ("AHP"). The Town has enacted a conflict of interest code requiring
certain public officials and employees in designated positions to report their financial interests on a
Statement of Economic Interests (Form 700). Among the positions designated in the Town's code
are members of the FAC, ADRB, and AHP.

The FAC's mission is to advise the City Council and Town staff on the fiscal operations and
financial stability of the Town by reviewing financial statements, audit reports and the budget
preparation process. FAC's typical responsibilities include reviewing Town financial statements,
audits, and investment programs; providing the City Council with recommendations on financial
matters; and reviewing new sources of revenue. In addition, the Town's investment policy must be
approved by FAC (and the City Council), and the Town's treasury operations are governed by said
policy. For this reason, the Town has determined that members of the FAC are officials who
manage public investments under Regulation 18700.3(b). Accordingly, the Town has not
designated FAC members under its conflict of interest code, but requires FAC members to disclose
their economic interests in accordance with Section 87200 of the Act.

The duties of the ADRB, meanwhile, are to consider and render decisions and make
recommendations to the City Council on applications and matters affecting the design of buildings,
structures, landscaping and other improvements, as well as adopt guidelines for the review of

File No. I-19-210
Page No. 3

applications. Members of the ADRB are designated in the Town's conflict of interest code and
required to report all interests in disclosure categories 1 and 2:

"Category 1: All investments and business positions in business entities, and sources of
income, including gifts, loans and travel payments."

"Category 2: All interests in real property which is located in whole or in part within, or not
more than two (2) miles outside, the boundaries of the Town."

Finally, AHP's mission is to develop and administer a program of education and policy
compliance overseeing the condition of public and private property in order to enhance the Town's
heritage and natural beauty, foster its legacy of public safety and sustain the value of property for
all residents. AHP's responsibilities include reviewing neighborhood complaints received by Code
Enforcement and the Building & Planning Department; issuing Decisions & Orders outlining
timeframes for homeowners to complete property projects to complete property projects; and
enforcing local, state, and federal regulations to promote the general welfare. Members of the AHP
are designated in the Town's conflict of interest code and required to report all interests in
disclosure categories 2 above, as well as 3 and 5:

"Category 3: All investments and business positions in business entities, and sources of
income, including gifts, loans and travel payments, that are engaged in land development,
construction or the acquisition or sale of real property within the jurisdiction of the town."

"Category 5: All investments and business positions in business entities, and sources of
income, including gifts, loans and travel payments, that provide services, products,
materials, machinery, vehicles or equipment of a type purchased or leased by the designated
position's department, unit, or division."

On a separate, but related question, in the Kasten Advice Letter (I-05-037), the Commission
previously advised the Town's mayor that he had to report investments in a high technology
company on his Form 700, even if that company only does business over the internet and has no
physical presence in the Town.

You now seek advice as to (1) whether the Town properly designated the Architecture &
Design Review Board (ADRB) and the Administrative Hearing Panel (AHP) in the Town's conflict
of interest code; (2) whether the Town properly classified volunteer members of its Financial
Advisory Committee (FAC) as Section 87200 filers; and (3) for public officials designated in the
Town's conflict of interest code, what constitutes a "business contract" for purposes of determining
an official's reportable investment interests.

ANALYSIS

The purpose of the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions is to ensure that public officials
"perform their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).) To this end,
every state and local agency must adopt and promulgate a conflict of interest code to help identify

File No. I-19-210
Page No. 4

potential conflicts of interest. (Section 87300.) A conflict of interest code must be formulated at the
most decentralized level possible (Section 87301); enumerate the positions within the agency, that
involve making or participation in the making of decisions which may have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on any financial interest (Section 87302(a)); and set forth the
specific types of investments, business positions, real property interests, or sources of income which
are reportable for each enumerated position (ibid).

Government Code Section 87200 further provides that, among other individuals, "other
public officials who manage public investments" must file statements of economic interests. Known
as statutory filers, public officials subject to Section 87200 are subject to full disclosure under the
Act. (See Sections 87206 through 87209.) Section 87200 is applicable to "other public officials who
manage public investments." The term "public official" includes "every member, officer, employee
or consultant of a state or local government agency." (Section 82048; Regulation 18700.)

1. Has the Town correctly designated the Architecture & Design Review Board (ADRB) and
the Administrative Hearing Panel (AHP) in the Town's conflict of interest code?

The first question is whether the Town has properly designated volunteer members of the
Architecture & Design Review Board (ADRB) and Administrative Hearing Panel (AHP),
respectively, in the Town's conflict of interest code. Section 82048 of the Act defines "public
official" as every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government agency.
For the purpose of further defining "public official," Regulation 18700(c)(2) defines "member" as
follows:

"(2) "Member" does not include an individual who performs duties as part of a committee,
board, commission, group, or other body that does not have decisionmaking authority.

"(A) A committee, board, commission, group, or other body possesses decisionmaking
authority whenever:

"(i) It may make a final governmental decision;

"(ii) It may compel or prevent a governmental decision either by reason of an exclusive
power to initiate the decision or by reason of a veto that may not be overriden; or

"(iii) It makes substantive recommendations and, over an extended period of time, those
recommendations have been regularly approved without significant amendment or
modification by another public official or governmental agency.

"(B) A committee, board, commission, group, or other body does not possess
decisionmaking authority if it is formed or engaged for the sole purpose of researching a
topic and preparing a report or recommendation for submission to another public official or
governmental agency that has final decisionmaking authority, and does not meet any of the
criteria set forth in subsection (2)(A)(i-iii)."

File No. I-19-210
Page No. 5

There is no question that ADRB and AHP are local government agencies. The next
question, then, is whether these bodies possess "decisionmaking authority," or whether these bodies
simply make recommendations to another agency that does have such authority, such as the City
Council.

Based on the facts provided, AHP appears to have decision-making authority. AHP's duties
include reviewing neighborhood complaints received by Code Enforcement and the Building &
Planning Department; issuing Decisions & Orders outlining timeframes for homeowners to
complete property projects. If AHP's decisions and orders are final, then AHP makes final
governmental decisions within the meaning of Regulation 18700 and its members should be
designated in the agency's conflict of interest code.

Similarly, the facts you have provided also suggest that ADRB has decision-making
authority and has been properly designated in the Town's conflict of interest code. ADRB's mission
is to consider and render decisions and make recommendations to the City Council on applications
and matters affecting the design of buildings, structures, landscaping and other improvements, as
well as to adopt guidelines for the review of applications." Assuming the decisions that ADRB
renders and the guidelines it adopts are final, ADRB is empowered to make final governmental
decisions within the meaning of Regulation 18700. Even if ADRB's decisions and guidelines are
not final, if the City Council routinely "rubber stamps" ADRB's design recommendations, ADRB is
also considered to have final decisionmaking authority. (Reg. 18700(c)(2)(A)(iii).) Therefore,
ADRB's members should be designated in the agency's conflict of interest code.

2. Has the Town properly classified volunteer members of its Financial Advisory Committee
(FAC) as Section 87200 filers?

Next, there is the question of the FAC, which the Town omitted from its conflict of interest
code, but made its members subject to full disclosure under Section 87200 of the Act, since the
Town found that FAC members are "other officials who manage public investments." Under
Regulation 18700.3(b)(1)(A), "other public officials who manage public investments is defined as:
"Members of boards and commissions [...] or committees, thereof, who exercise responsibility for
the management of public investments." "Public investments" means the investment of public
moneys in real estate, securities, or other economic interests for the production of revenue or other
financial return. (Reg. 18700.3(c).) "Public moneys" means all money belonging to, received by, or
held by, the state, or any city, county, town, district, or public agency therein." (Reg. 18700.3(d).)
And "management of public investments means the following nonministerial functions: directing
the investment of public moneys; formulating or approving investment policies; approving or
establishing guidelines for asset allocations; or approving investment transactions." (Regulation
18700.3(e).)

Members of the FAC appear to meet the criteria of "other public officials who manage
public investments" under Regulation 18700.3. FAC is a local government committee whose duties
include management of public investments, namely formulating or approving investment policies
by reviewing the Town's investment program and approving the Town's investment policy and
reviewing new sources of revenue. As described above, FAC's duties are related to public
investments, namely to advise the City Council and Town staff on the fiscal operations and

File No. 1-19-210
Page No. 6

financial stability of the Town by reviewing financial statements, audit reports and the budget
preparation process. FAC's typical responsibilities include reviewing Town financial statements,
audits, and investment programs; providing the City Council with recommendations on financial
matters; and reviewing new sources of revenue. In addition, the Town's conflict of interest code
tasks the FAC with approving the Town's investment policy. Thus, members of the FAC are
properly classified as Section 87200 filers.


3. For public officials designated in the Town's conflict of interest code, what constitutes a
"business contact" for purposes of determining an official's reportable investment
interests?

Finally, you seek advice on when companies, such as large-scale providers of consumer
goods or services, most of which are acquired via the internet or physically purchased outside the
jurisdiction of the Town, are "doing business" within the Town's jurisdiction for purposes of
determining whether a designated official must report investments in companies, including stocks,
on the official's Statement of Economic Interests (Form 700).

As you noted, in the Kasten Advice Letter, No. I-05-037, we advised the Town's mayor that
he was required to report an investment in Dell, Inc. on his Form 700, even though Dell, Inc.
conducts business via the internet only, since "Dell, Inc. obviously does business in Hillsborough
by selling its products and services to residents and others located in the city." This advice remains
valid.

The Act's conflict of interest rules prohibit a public official from making, participating in
making, or using his or her official position in any way to influence a governmental decision in
which the official knows or has reason to know that he or she has a "financial interest." (Section
87100.) In addition, certain state and local public officials must file periodic Statements of
Economic Interests (Form 700) disclosing those personal assets and interests that may be affected
during the performance of their official duties. (Sections 87200 - 87350.) As discussed above,
public officials who are required to file Statements of Economic Interests are either identified in
Section 87200 (statutory filers) or designated in an agency's conflict-of-interest code (designated
employees or code filers). (Sections 87300 and 87302.)

The Act only requires officials to report sources of income that are located in or doing
business in the jurisdiction of the official's agency. (Section 82030(a).) Regulation 18230 explicitly
defines "doing business in the jurisdiction" as "if that person has business contacts on a regular or
substantial basis with a person who maintains a physical presence in the jurisdiction of a public
official." Regulation 18230 further provides that "'Business contacts' include, but are not limited
to, manufacturing, distributing, selling, purchasing, or providing services or goods," but notes that
"'Business contacts' do not include marketing via the Internet, telephone, radio, or
printed media."

Thus, as we advised in the Kasten Advice Letter, "simply because a business entity has a
website that is accessible to those in Hillsborough with internet accessibility is not enough to
qualify the business entity as "doing business in the jurisdiction." However, also as advised in the

File No. 1-19-210
Page No. 7

Kasten Advice Letter, "if a company does business on a regular and substantial basis with a resident
or other persons with a physical presence in the Town, it would be enough to trigger 'doing
business in the jurisdiction.'" The same advice applies today. Even if a large-scale provider of
consumer goods over the internet, such as an e-retailer, does not have a physical presence in the
jurisdiction in the form of a store front or office in the Town, if the retailer sells goods and services
to residents on a regular basis, such contacts would be sufficient to be considered "doing business in
the jurisdiction" of Hillsborough. By contrast, if an official designated in the Town's conflict of
interest code (or an 87200 filer) owns stock in a brick and mortar retailer based, for example, in
another state or country, which only does business outside of California, such an investment would
not be reportable.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

[signature]

By: Toren A. Lewis
Counsel, Legal Division

TAL:aja

Kasten Advice Letter, "if a company does business on a regular and substantial basis with a resident 
or other persons with a physical presence in the Town, it would be enough to trigger 'doing 
business in the jurisdiction."' The same advice applies today. Even if a large-scale provider of 
consumer goods over the internet, such as an e-retailer, does not have a physical presence in the 
jurisdiction in the form of a store front or office in the Town, if the retailer sells goods and services 
to residents on a regular basis, such contacts would be sufficient to be considered "doing business in 
the jurisdiction" of Hillsborough. By contrast, if an official designated in the Town's conflict of 
interest code ( or an 87200 filer) owns stock in a brick and mortar retailer based, for example, in 
another state or country, which only does business outside of California, such an investment would 
not be reportable.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. Has the Town properly designated the Architecture & Design Review Board (ADRB) and 
the Administrative Hearing Panel (AHP) in the Town's conflict of interest code? 
2. Has the Town properly classified volunteer members of its Financial Advisory Committee 
(F AC) as Section 87200 filers? 
3. For public officials designated in the Town's conflict of interest code, what constitutes2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal 
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).) 


FileNo. I-19-210  
a "business contact" for purposes of determining an official's reportable investment 
interests?

CONCLUSION: 1. Yes. Because the ADRB and the AHP possess decision-making authority, their members 
must be designated in their agency conflict of interest codes. 
2. Yes. Because the F AC is comprised of officials who manage public investments, F AC 
members are properly classified as Section 87200 filers. 
3. While "business contacts" do not include merely marketing via the Internet, telephone, 
television, radio, or printed media, if a company does business on a regular and substantial 
basis with a resident or other persons with a physical presence in the Town, this could 
constitute "doing business in the jurisdiction."

Diaz City Attorney Town of Hillsborough for BEST BEST & KRIEGER LLP 2001 N. Main Street, Suite 390 Walnut Creek, CA 94596 Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. 1-19-210 Dear Mr. Diaz: This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act") on behalf of the Town of Hillsborough ("Town").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest code and Statement of Economic Interest reporting provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. Please note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice. Because your inquiry is general in nature, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.² QUESTIONS 1. Has the Town properly designated the Architecture & Design Review Board (ADRB) and the Administrative Hearing Panel (AHP) in the Town's conflict of interest code? 2. Has the Town properly classified volunteer members of its Financial Advisory Committee (FAC) as Section 87200 filers? 3. For public officials designated in the Town's conflict of interest code, what constitutes ___________________________ ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. ² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).) File No. I-19-210 Page No. 2 a "business contact" for purposes of determining an official's reportable investment interests? CONCLUSIONS 1. Yes. Because the ADRB and the AHP possess decision-making authority, their members must be designated in their agency conflict of interest codes. 2. Yes. Because the FAC is comprised of officials who manage public investments, FAC members are properly classified as Section 87200 filers. 3. While "business contacts" do not include merely marketing via the Internet, telephone, television, radio, or printed media, if a company does business on a regular and substantial basis with a resident or other persons with a physical presence in the Town, this could constitute "doing business in the jurisdiction."
